results.title: Visme is rated "Average" with 3.1 / 5 on Trustpilot
results.content:
When I contacted support about these clear failures, they refused to acknowledge the AI doesn't work as advertised, denied any refund despite the service being fundamentally broken, hid behind their terms and conditions rather than fixing obvious problems

Showed zero interest in resolving legitimate technical failures. In essence i paid 60 dollar to trial it for on hour only to find it did not in any way offer what was claimed. I had researched extensively and was very disappointed that this service just did not work. It offer a range of basic templates and all their AI does it upload your words into the template.

If I tried to suggest colours it crashed, if i tried to upload something it crashed. [...] May 25, 2026

Image 36: Rated 1 out of 5 stars

## Very bad UX and other platforms offer…
Very bad UX and other platforms offer better features for better price. Also they will auto renew your subscription without your permission. I advice to remove the payment details from the account, and don't use it, search for better options

results.title: Founders use AI like a team not an intern | Marina Mogilko posted on the topic | LinkedIn
results.content:
Image 6: Asaad Hasan, graphic

Asaad Hasan 3w 

   Report this comment

The intern vs. team framing nails it. The bottleneck isn't the AI it's whether the founder can map their process clearly enough to hand it off. That's the real skill: process clarity, not prompt craft. The people who figure that out early aren't just more productive. They're building institutional knowledge that compounds.

results.title: WizeMe.APP | Private AI Continuity and Decision OS
results.content:
## Trust starts with boundaries.

WizeMe should say when evidence is not loaded, keep connected sources permissioned and source-labeled, and avoid pretending certainty from a cold start. Public benchmark artifacts and docs are published separately from private product internals so claims can be checked without exposing user data, source code, training corpora, API keys, environment files, or internal secrets. [...] Private continuity for people carrying too much context

# Stop losing the thread. WizeMe remembers what matters so you move.

One private partner turns scattered apps, meetings, notes, relationships, and open loops into clearer decisions and follow-through.

Scattered
meetings
decisions
people
routines
become remembered next moves.

Start here

## Bring one real thing.

A decision, meeting, relationship, habit, or open loop. WizeMe turns it into a cleaner next move before asking you to learn the product.

## Stop making every app, meeting, note, and open loop compete for your attention. [...] Most productivity tools store fragments. WizeMe is designed to turn those fragments into remembered decisions, next moves, relationship context, routines, and useful briefs. The point is not another dashboard. The point is a private partner that helps you carry context forward without making your work or life feel managed by a machine.

## How WizeMe helps

### 1. Bring one real thing

Start with a decision, goal, person, meeting, habit, health signal, note, or pressure point. WizeMe uses a guided first session when you are new and a direct app path when you are returning.

### 2. Build continuity

Your partner can remember decisions, constraints, corrections, people, and outcomes with permission, then use that context to make the next conversation sharper.

### 3. Follow through

I don’t care what’s on either end of my current playing track, or at least show it in a way that doesn’t take up artwork space. The album art is always an awesome part of the music’s personality so it shouldn’t be minimised like this.
Also the now playing bar at the bottom of the screen isn’t ﬂat looking, looks like design from windows XP. Not happy. An awesome service needs an awesome interface.

results.title: 4 User Experience Issues Harming your Mobile App’s Ratings (according to our big research of angrrry reviews) — Panaxeo
results.content:
#### 04. A Not-So-Smart Home App

The problem: More often than not, a “smart home” app designed to connect to your washing machine, oven or fridge ends up being less than stellar. Sometimes the app takes literal days to connect to the device, other times it decides to remain disconnected forever. Come on! Days when broken bluetooth connections were acceptable are so 2010; today, consumers demand better. Even vacuum applications aren’t allowed to suck.

A robot vacuum app might decide to delete its saved flat data every week or so, leading the poor owner to frustration. Nobody enjoys having to scan their flat anew four times a month. The robot itself may be fine, but what does it matter if the app isn’t.

The solution: Bring users to the “construction site” [...] #### 01. Bad Visual Design and Poor User Flow

The problem: An app has to make a good first impression, otherwise it is doomed to fail. Best case scenario, the user simply uninstalls the unimpressive, unintuitive app. But more often than not, they get so frustrated with confusing menus, messy fonts and ugly colours, they opt to leave a ranty review, harming the app in the long run. You’ll want to avoid such a scenario. No matter how useful and practical your app is, do make sure it also looks and works the part.

The solution: Stick to what works

And by that we’re not saying you shouldn’t be innovative! [...] Stability is vital and ties in closely with UX. Especially with loyalty apps, the customer needs to be able to get their phone out quickly, open the app, scan the code and be done with it. Poor stability just leads to frustration with the app, with your brand, and subsequently ends up in the customer looking for someone who just does it better.

The solution: Monitor your crashes
